Category: Education & Public Sector: Education, Government Services, Non-Profit organizations
Description: Risks in resource allocation and impact assessment.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how your non-profit organisation operates, including general awareness of budgeting and programme planning, is all you need to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key risks associated with using AI in resource allocation and explain how these risks can affect decision making within non-profit organisations. You will also be able to apply practical frameworks to assess the impact of AI-driven decisions, helping your organisation prioritise ethical and effective outcomes.

lessons.Description: This lesson introduces non-profit professionals to the ways artificial intelligence can support and complicate decision making within their organisations. You will explore how AI tools are being used to allocate limited resources more efficiently and to assess the impact of programmes and services. Along the way, we will examine the real risks that come with relying on AI, including issues around bias, data quality, and accountability. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of how to approach AI thoughtfully, so that it serves your mission rather than undermining it. No prior technical knowledge is required, just an openness to exploring how these tools might apply to your work.
lessons.content: Risks in resource allocation and impact assessment.

slides.subtitle: Exploring AI risks in non-profit decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools are increasingly used by non-profits to guide decisions on funding, staffing, and programmes
• This course focuses on the risks
• Poor AI decisions can misdirect limited resources and harm the communities you serve
• No prior technical knowledge required
• Real examples drawn from resource allocation and impact assessment challenges

slides.subtitle: Where AI Decisions Can Go Wrong
slides.slidecontent:
• Biased data skews decisions
• Errors misallocate resources
• Accountability gaps emerge
• Impact scores mislead staff
• Vulnerable groups face harm

slides.subtitle: Where AI Decisions Can Go Wrong
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools trained on biased data may consistently underfund certain communities or overlooked groups
• Automated decisions can lack the human context needed to reflect real-world need
• Over-reliance on historical patterns
• Impact assessments driven by AI may miss qualitative outcomes that matter most to beneficiaries
• Small errors in data input can lead to large, compounding misallocations over time

slides.subtitle: Recognising Patterns That Skew Results
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools trained on historical data can reflect past inequalities in funding and service delivery
• Outputs favouring certain demographics over others without clear justification
• Watch for skewed results
• Recommendations that consistently overlook rural, minority, or under-resourced communities
• Checking whether training data represents your actual beneficiary population
• A result that looks neutral on the surface can still embed structural bias

slides.subtitle: Reducing Bias and Allocation Errors Effectively
slides.slidecontent:
• Regularly audit AI outputs for patterns of unfair resource distribution across different community groups
• Test with diverse data
• Involve frontline staff in reviewing AI recommendations before any final allocation decisions are made
• Cross-check AI suggestions against your organisation's own impact records and beneficiary feedback
• Document every override
• Build in a clear review cycle so allocation models are updated as community needs change over time

slides.subtitle: Recognising and Reducing Algorithmic Prejudice
slides.slidecontent:
• Biased data skews results
• Past inequity repeats digitally
• Audit outputs regularly
• Diverse teams spot errors
• Question surprising recommendations

slides.subtitle: Practical Steps for Safer AI Use
slides.slidecontent:
• Audit AI outputs regularly
• Train staff on bias
• Keep humans in control
• Document all AI decisions
• Review data sources often

slides.subtitle: Core principles for responsible AI use
slides.slidecontent:
• AI supports, not replaces, decisions
• Check data for bias regularly
• Keep humans accountable always
• Start small, then scale up
• Review AI outputs critically
• Next step: pilot one AI tool

scripts.script: Welcome, and thank you for joining this course on AI in non-profit decision making. Over the past few years, AI tools have found their way into all sorts of organisational decisions, and non-profits are no exception. From allocating funding to managing staffing and designing programmes, these tools are being used in ways that can have a real impact on the communities you serve. Now, this course is not here to celebrate that trend. We are going to focus specifically on the risks, because when AI gets things wrong in a non-profit context, the consequences can be serious. Limited resources get misdirected, and the people who depend on your organisation can end up worse off. The good news is that you do not need any technical background to follow along. Everything will be explained in plain language, and we will be drawing on real examples from areas like resource allocation and impact assessment. So let us get started.

So let's talk about where things can actually go wrong when non-profits start using AI to make decisions. The first thing to be aware of is that AI systems learn from historical data, and if that data contains bias, the AI will simply repeat and reinforce those biases in its outputs. That can lead to resources being directed to the wrong places, or certain communities being overlooked entirely. There's also a real question of accountability. When an AI system makes a poor recommendation, it isn't always clear who is responsible for that mistake. And here's something that catches a lot of organisations off guard: AI tools often produce scores or ratings that look very precise and authoritative, but staff can misinterpret what those numbers actually mean. Perhaps most importantly, the people most at risk from these errors are often the most vulnerable individuals your organisation exists to support. So understanding these risks isn't just a technical concern, it's a fundamental part of responsible practice.

So, we've seen how AI can help with resource allocation, but let's be honest about where things can go wrong. One of the biggest concerns is bias. If the data used to train an AI tool reflects historical inequalities, the system will likely reproduce those same inequalities, meaning certain communities could consistently miss out on funding. There's also the issue of context. AI doesn't understand nuance the way a person does, so it can miss the very human factors that tell you where support is genuinely needed most. And if a system is heavily influenced by past patterns, it may struggle to respond to new or changing circumstances. On top of that, qualitative outcomes, things like improved wellbeing or community confidence, are often invisible to AI assessments. Finally, even a small error in your data can snowball into significant misallocations over time. These aren't reasons to avoid AI, but they are absolutely reasons to use it carefully.

One of the most important things to understand about AI tools is that they learn from historical data, and that data often reflects the inequalities that already exist in our sector. So if past funding decisions consistently overlooked rural communities or minority groups, an AI system trained on that data may quietly reproduce those same patterns. The tricky part is that the output can look perfectly reasonable on the surface. There are no obvious red flags, no alarming language, just a clean recommendation that happens to favour certain groups over others. What you want to watch for is consistency. If the same communities keep getting deprioritised in the results, that is worth questioning. A good starting point is to ask whether the data the tool was trained on actually reflects the people you serve. If your beneficiaries are not well represented in that training data, the results will not serve them well either.

So, once you're using AI to help with resource allocation, the real work begins in making sure it stays fair and accurate over time. The most important habit is regular auditing. Look at the outputs and ask: are certain groups consistently receiving less? If so, that's a signal something needs adjusting. Make sure the data you're feeding into the system reflects the full diversity of the communities you serve. And importantly, don't let AI make final decisions alone. Bring your frontline staff into the process. They'll often spot things the algorithm simply cannot. It's also worth cross-checking AI recommendations against your own records and beneficiary feedback, because your organisation holds knowledge that no model can replicate. Whenever you override a recommendation, write it down and explain why. That documentation builds a valuable evidence trail. Finally, set a regular review cycle so your allocation model keeps pace as community needs evolve.

Now, one of the most important things to understand about AI is that it is only as fair as the data it learns from. If that data reflects historical inequalities, such as funding patterns that consistently overlooked certain communities, the AI will simply repeat those patterns and present them as objective recommendations. That is a real risk, and it is why you cannot just set up an AI tool and trust it blindly. You need to regularly review the outputs it produces, looking for anything that seems skewed or unexpected. If a recommendation surprises you, dig into it and ask why. It is also worth making sure that the people reviewing AI outputs come from diverse backgrounds, because different perspectives are far better at catching subtle errors or unfair assumptions. Essentially, AI can reinforce prejudice without anyone intending it to, so building in human oversight and critical thinking is absolutely essential for responsible use in your organisation.

So now that we understand the risks, let's talk about what you can actually do to manage them. First, make it a habit to regularly check the outputs your AI tools are producing. Are they making sense? Are they fair? Don't just trust them blindly. Alongside that, make sure your team understands what bias is and how it can creep into AI systems, because awareness really is your first line of defence. Crucially, always keep a human being in the decision-making loop. AI should be informing your choices, not making them for you. It's also really good practice to keep a clear record of when and how AI has been used in any decision, so you have a proper audit trail if questions arise later. And finally, revisit the data sources your AI tools are drawing from on a regular basis, because data can become outdated or unreliable over time. Small, consistent habits like these can make a significant difference.

So let's bring everything together from this lesson. The most important thing to hold onto is that AI is here to support your judgement, not to replace it. You and your team remain responsible for every decision made in your organisation. As you begin exploring AI tools, keep a close eye on the data you're feeding into them, because bias in data leads to biased outputs, and that can cause real harm. Start small, perhaps with just one process or one tool, and build your confidence before scaling up. And whenever AI gives you a result or a recommendation, approach it with a critical eye. Ask yourself whether it makes sense, whether it reflects your values, and whether it serves your beneficiaries well. On that note, your practical next step is simply to identify one AI tool and run a small pilot with it. Small, thoughtful steps are how responsible AI adoption begins.
